text: Following the transection of the proximal and distal sides of the intestinal tract using a laparoscopic stapler, the surgeon injects endocyanine green intravenously. Laparoscopic near-infrared imaging confirms good blood flow in the anastomosis area.
The surgeon creates ten millimeter transverse enterotomies at the mesenteric edges of the stapling lines on both the transverse colon and descending colon.
The surgeon inserts each jaw of a 45mm linear stapler into the holes along the posterior wall of both intestinal tracts, closes the device, and fires it to create the anastomosis.
Following the initial suturing of the intestinal tracts, the surgeon inspects the interior of the anastomosis site to ensure there is no visible bleeding.
The assistant holds the proximal and distal ends of the first suture line as the surgeon prepares to close the entry hole in the intestinal tract.

The surgeon uses an Endo GIA reinforced reload to grasp the ends of the suture line. The entry hole is partially closed with a 30mm stapler, followed by complete closure with a 60mm stapler. The initial use of the 30mm stapler prevents anastomosis stenosis, and dividing the closure into two steps eliminates the need for suturing.
The surgeon uses a sixty millimeters linear stapler to completely close the entry hole on the second firing, ensuring that all layers of the intestinal tract are securely sutured.

The surgeon completes the intracorporeal sutureless stapled end-to-end anastomosis, ensuring the mesentery orientation is consistent on both the proximal and distal sides. The anastomosis site is visually inspected, confirming the completion of the procedure within eleven minutes.
